title: Budget Modifications Process: Conceptual Budgeting, Modifications Tracking, and Budget vs. Actual
summary: Erin Stetzer walked participants through her three-part budgeting framework: the conceptual budget (including the critical 'price certainty' column), the modifications tracking process, and the budget versus actual spreadsheet used throughout construction. The session generated extensive discussion around how these tools fit into the sales process, how to handle owner-supplied materials, and how to structure client communication and fees on cost-plus projects.

teachings.explanation: Adding a column to the conceptual budget that communicates how certain the builder is about each line item forces honest, transparent conversations with clients about the reliability of numbers before work begins. It prevents clients from being surprised later and builds trust by demonstrating thorough research. Erin credits this single column with transforming her business and her ability to convert clients.
teachings.quote: The most important column in this conceptual budget is what we call the price certainty. This column alone is based off of two years of me not getting projects because I didn't realize I was the one telling the truth and maybe others were not telling the truth and people were surprised.

teachings.concept: Conceptual Budget as a Living, Never-Final Document
teachings.explanation: The conceptual budget is intentionally named 'conceptual' because construction budgets are always in flux until 100% of information is known. Framing the budget as inherently human and dynamic helps set client expectations and opens the door for ongoing communication rather than locking into a false sense of finality.
teachings.quote: The next phase from conceptual budget to starting is not a final budget. It is taking a conceptual budget and implanting it into a system and it will change and it will move and it's human. The process is human.

teachings.concept: Budget vs. Actual with Percent Complete Tracking
teachings.explanation: During construction, every invoice and draw request must be cross-referenced against actual physical progress on the job site. If the financial percent drawn does not match the construction percent complete, something is wrong. This discipline prevents overbilling by trades and keeps the project financially healthy.
teachings.quote: Construction needs to match these percentages. Whenever we get invoices, we match construction to percent complete. If the bottom plate is in and they're charging me 31% on the project, that invoice goes right back to them.

teachings.concept: Selling Through Systems, Not Through Price
teachings.explanation: Rather than competing on fees or justifying margins directly, Erin presents a proposal booklet that showcases her operational systems—weekly reports, four-week calendars, budget tracking, and communication protocols. By the time fees are discussed, clients already see the value delivered, making price negotiation largely unnecessary.
teachings.quote: The fees really aren't a hard justification because it's pretty clear that we're probably the most expensive person you're talking to. We do what we say we're going to do. You're going to get an exceptional home.

teachings.concept: Requiring a Pricing Set Before Estimating
teachings.explanation: Erin refuses to provide any budget figures without a minimum 'pricing set' that includes floor plans, exterior elevations, cut sections, and allowances. This positions the builder as a professional who needs adequate information to provide professional feedback, rather than someone who reacts to incomplete data.
teachings.quote: In order for us to provide professional feedback, the pricing set needs to include the floor plans, the exterior elevations, cut sections, send it to me. I'll let you know if we need more information, specifications, allowances. That's what's required before we look at it.

teachings.concept: Modifications Log as an Internal Tracking and Client Communication Tool
teachings.explanation: Every change—whether billable to the client or an internal correction—is logged in a modifications log. The total potential dollar value of all pending modifications should be surfaced at every owner-architect-contractor (OAC) meeting so clients understand the full financial picture, not just what has already been approved.
teachings.quote: It's a section on our owner, our OAC meeting agenda. It's right there at the top of the list. So it's touched every time we get together.

teachings.concept: Non-Negotiable Fee Structure Backed by Delivery
teachings.explanation: Erin's approach is to set fees based on the real cost of delivering at her standards and not negotiate them down. If clients push back, she explains the systems and value behind the fee. If they remain unwilling, she moves on rather than compromising the business model.
teachings.quote: If this is what it takes to operate at this level and these standards, period. And it is what it is. I know our systems. I know our delivery. I'm not negotiating. If you want to work with us, let's work together. If you don't want to work with us, there are a kabillion builders that you should talk to.

teachings.concept: Architects and Designers Are Responsible for Their Own Allowances
teachings.explanation: In order to maintain professional accountability across the project team, architects and designers should provide their own allowances (e.g., for windows, countertops) rather than having the builder guess. Builders who absorb this responsibility take on risk they cannot control.
teachings.quote: In order to be helpful, people need to be responsible for providing information for the team.

actionable_advice: Add a 'price certainty' column (or equivalent color-coded stoplight system) to every conceptual budget to communicate confidence levels on each line item before presenting to clients.
Do not provide any cost estimates until you have a minimum pricing set that includes floor plans, exterior elevations, cut sections, specifications, and allowances.
Use the budget vs. actual spreadsheet every week internally, and send an updated version to clients each time a draw is requested, adding a new column for each draw.
Cross-reference every trade invoice against actual physical progress on the job site before approving payment; reject invoices where the percent billed does not match percent of work complete.
Surface the full total of pending modifications at every OAC meeting so clients understand the potential financial impact of decisions still in progress, not just approved changes.
Require modifications to go through a formal approval sheet process—written up, sent to the client, signed via DocuSign or similar—before being logged and incorporated into the budget.

Present a proposal booklet to prospective clients that explains your systems (weekly reports, four-week calendars, budget tracking, communication protocols) before discussing fees, so the value is clear.
Structure client meetings on a strict cadence (e.g., once per week on-site with budget and weekly report in hand) and set that expectation upfront so clients know when and how to communicate.
When working without project managers, block calendar time for each project site visit and administrative work, and communicate availability windows clearly to clients and trades.
For owner-supplied materials, have an explicit contract clause assigning full responsibility—including damage, delays, and installation coordination—to the client or designer supplying the materials.
client_challenges: Participants were unsure how the conceptual budget fits into the overall sales process and when it transitions into the working construction budget.

Builders expressed difficulty knowing what minimum information (pricing set) is needed before they can responsibly produce a conceptual budget, especially when clients approach them before any plans exist.
Participants struggled with how to handle owner-supplied or designer-supplied materials on cost-plus projects, including who bears responsibility for damage, delays, and coordination.
There was confusion about whether the percent complete column in the budget vs. actual spreadsheet reflects financial draws or physical construction progress.
Participants asked how to structure client-facing communication around the conceptual budget—whether clients see it in real time, receive PDFs, or only view it at formal meetings.

A participant building new construction asked how to mentally and operationally plan for the timeline from first budget conversation to contract signing to groundbreaking across multiple pipeline projects.
A participant asked how to manage all budgeting, client communication, and site oversight without a project management team—specifically how to structure their week as a solo operator.
Participants debated whether and how to show profit as a line item in the conceptual budget, given that clients on large projects often scrutinize margins.
notable_quotes: On price certainty: 'I just said, forget it. I will always tell the truth and I'm going to add a column. And so now when we have conversations with people, whether they work with us or not, they understand what these numbers actually mean.'
On the human nature of budgets: 'Unless I have 100% of the information, this is controlled chaos. That's my personal approach. So there's never anything ever final in my world.'

On not playing the hypothetical game with clients: 'We don't operate on hypotheticals. And this budget includes hypotheticals. We're providing our insight, our historical data. When we do get the actual information, then I say, this is what it is. Do we want to move forward with it?'

On professionalism and fees: 'People would never ask their attorney or their physician, what's your margin on my job? How many other industries out there would even be willing to share what your profit is?'
On walking away from projects: 'Any project I've ever gotten or didn't get, I've never driven past that home. It's literally behind me. What's next?'

On the price certainty column's business impact: 'I put the price certainty column in and I'm not kidding you, it literally changed our entire business. It shows that you did your homework, you know this project better than anyone, and you're able to engage in an active conversation because you've done the research.'
